title: An ion conducted tour through some polymer electrolytes
abstract:
Polymer International 47 (1998) 20È27
An Ion Conducted Tour Through Some
Polymer Electrolytes
J. M. G. Cowie
Department of Chemistry, Heriot-Watt University, Edinburgh EH14 4AS, UK
(Received 23 October 1997; accepted 21 January 1998)
Abstract: The development of both dry polymer electrolytes and gel or inert-
support polymer electrolytes is discussed and their performances compared. Con-
ductivities have been measured for comb-branch polymers, with cation-binding
side-chains, as a function of temperature and in nearly every case the response
was non-Arrhenius. This can be explained by recognizing that ion transport in
dry polymer matrices is a function of the Ñexibility of the polymer chains at the
temperature of measurement. Ways to maximize this by attempting to lower the
glass transition temperature of the polymer/salt mixtures and improve the ion-
ization of the salt, are described. The active participation of the polymer chain in
ion transport can be modiÐed by moving to externally plasticized systems, gels,
or inert polymer supports. Two such examples are described, b-cyclodextrin/
dimethylacetamide/salt gels and highly porous polyethylene support Ðlms loaded
with electrolyte. These show a reduced dependence of conductivity (p) with
change in temperature (T ) which is a desirable feature in device manufacture, and
an Arrhenius (log pÈ1/T ) behaviour. While these systems have several attractive
features and they have been designed to minimize solvent leakage, there is no
guarantee that leakage will not occur over a period of time and this limitation
must be recognized.
1998 Society of Chemical Industry
(
Polym. Int. 47, 20È27 (1998)
Key words: polymer electrolytes; polymer gels; conductivity; comb-branch
polymers
INTRODUCTION
The Ðrst reported observations on ionic conductivity in
polymeric media were made by Wright and co-workers,
1,2 but it was not until Armand et al.3 suggested that
polymer electrolytes could be used in applications such
as dry batteries that interest in this area expanded. This
led rapidly to the recognition that if useful, solvent free,
polymer electrolytes were to be developed, certain cri-
teria had to be met. These can be summarized as
follows: (a) the polymer host must be capable of dis-
solving (ionizing) the added salt to form a one-phase
solution; (b) ionic conduction occurs through the
amorphous phase of the polymer so polymeric media
should be non-crystalline; (c) the mechanism of ion con-
duction involves liquid-like relaxation processes of the
polymer chains, so systems with low glass transition
temperatures
are preferred because they are more
(Tg)
likely to exhibit the rapid relaxations at ambient tem-
peratures which facilitate ion transport; (d) the systems
should be mechanically strong and electrochemically
stable.
Polymers that are able to dissolve salts e†ectively
usually contain oxygen, nitrogen or sulphur, because
these atoms are capable of co-ordinating metal cations
and thereby ionizing, at least partially, the component
salt to produce the individual ionic charge carriers. A
wide range of polymers with suitable heteroatomic
structures
have
been
studied4
but
the
archetypal
polymer is poly(ethylene oxide) (PEO). This has the
most suitable structure to ensure the efficient, co-
ordination of alkali metal cations, and in particular Li`,
which is of special interest in the context of lithium bat-
teries. Unfortunately PEO is a semi-crystalline polymer
which impairs the ionic conductivity p unless used at
temperatures
above
its
crystalline
melting
point.

However, several strategies have been developed to
overcome this deÐciency and produce wholly amorph-
ous systems. These include formation of block or
random copolymers,5 crosslinked network structures,6
and the use of comb-branch polymers with oligomeric,
ethylene oxide7h10 or propylene oxide11 side chains.
COMB-BRANCH POLY(ITACONATE)S AND
POLYETHERS
Our own interest in this area stemmed from an exten-
sive study of comb-branch polymers, many based on the
itaconic acid unit, which led to the synthesis of comb-
branch structures with short PEO side-chains of con-
trolled and varied lengths.12 These are amorphous
materials with low
values, depending on the length of
Tg
the side-chain, which plasticizes (internally) the rela-
tively sti† poly(itaconate) backbone 1.
The ionic conductivities for poly(itaconate)s with oli-
gomeric ethylene oxide 1(a) and propylene oxide 1(b)
side chains were measured as a function of temperature.
Comparison of the behaviour of samples with similar
side-chain length and added salt concentration indic-
ated that series 1(a)13 had higher conductivities than
samples 1(b)11 and this was found to be a consequence
of the superior complexing abilities of the ethylene
oxide side-chains for cations. It was demonstrated14
that the bulkier propylene oxide units were unable to
form tight co-ordinating shells around the cations and
so salt-ionization was poorer.
Even better levels of conductivity could be attained
when a series of polyethers 2 were prepared.15
These had lower initial
values and correspondingly
Tg
higher conductivities. However, accumulated data indic-
ated that, in general, the levels of conductivity depended
on the side chain length, the temperature and the salt
concentration.

conductivity improves as the sample side-chain length
increases. This phenomenon could be a result of two
factors. One is the observation that crown ethers are
very

cation
binding
structures.
Crown-4,
crown-5 and crown-6 are strong binding agents for Li`,
Na` and K`, respectively, and so the linear, side-chain,
ethylene oxide (EO) analogues (m \ 4È6) used in (1a)
should be the most efficient side-chain complexing units.
Once side-chains with a maximum of four-oxygen
atoms are used, the co-ordinating abilities should be
similar for longer EO sequence side-chains and the
second factorÈsample

the higher the
of the polymer/salt solution becomes.

equilibrium process:

where P is the polymer and MX the salt, and this
showed that the ionization process was not efficient. It
was calculated that the ionization was only about 60%
at low concentration, which fell to about 20% as more
salt was added. Nevertheless the e†ect on

cant and often quite large (see Fig. 2). Unfortunately,

and 3. Conductivity increases as the temperature is

While this maximum appears to move with an increase

For comb-branch systems, the mechanical strength of
the polymer/salt Ðlms can be stabilized and ensured by
crosslinking the system lightly.19 Crosslinking normally
tends to restrict chain Ñexibility, but if levels are kept
below 10% there is no loss of overall conductivity as
can be seen on inspection of the data in Fig. 7. This
shows the log p versus 1/T data with 2%, 5% and 11%
crosslinking, where there is no loss of conductivity until

While much e†ort has been expended by many groups
in attempting to produce higher conductivity levels in
totally “dryÏ systems, by lowering the
materials, using new salts with better “hardÈsoft ion
combinations to encourage more e†ective ionization,
and blending with higher dielectric polymeric media,
there seem to be inherent limitations to how far one can
improve the conductivity values. The best that can be
achieved are room temperature conductivities of 10~3È
10~4 S cm~1 and perhaps an order of magnitude better
at about 373 K, but higher ambient conductivities are
A strategy explored in this laboratory in an attempt to
promote better salt ionization was to prepare comb-
branch poly(crown ethers).20,21 Structures such as 3, 4
Fig. 8. Temperature dependence of conductivity for structure
and typical conductivity data are shown in Figs 8 and 9.
The “bestÏ values of conductivity were obtained for
structures in which the crown ether was attached to a
Ñexible polymer backbone by equally Ñexible “spacerÏ
by internal plasticization of the
starting material. Addition of salt again leads to an
in some cases even more than for the
linear side-chain analogues. This is caused by the
increased rigidity of the crown structure when complex-
ed by a cation and also through ring/cation/anion inter-
actions, producing transient crosslinking in the matrix,
which hinders chain relaxation processes.
Results also tend to suggest that, as the highest con-
ductivities were obtained when the cationÈcrown ether
interaction was maximized, the cationÈring interaction
was probably too strong to allow easy mobility of the
cation and that the anions became the major contrib-
utors to the conductivity. As cation conduction is norm-
ally required for many of the applications, there is some
interest in single-ion conductors, and these have also
Data for a (15-crown-5) structure n \ 1
Several authors22h27 have reported work on single-ion
conducting systems in which the anion, usually the sul-
phonate group, is either bonded covalently to the
polymer chain or the anion is made so large that its
mobility is severely restricted.
The approach used here was to synthesize a new Ñuoro-
sulphonate anion which might allow easier release of the
cation for conduction.28 Monomers 6, 7 and 8 were
synthesized and copolymerized with 9 which acts as the
and the copolymer composition was adjusted to give
the required [cation]/[O] ratio. The
of the samples were measured and compared with those
for homopolymer 9 mixed with lithium triÑate. It was
found that for comparable [Li`]/[O] ratios, the Tg

might surmise that the sulphonate anion bonded to the
polymer chain cannot participate in the interchain cou-
lombic bonding as e†ectively as a free anion, which is
believed to act as a bridge between cations complexed
by the polymer. Thus these transient crosslinks, which
restrict chain motion and raise
oped in the copolymers comprising 6 and 7. The tighter
binding of the Li` to the carboxylate anion may simply
increase steric hindrance to chain rotation and cause
to increase in these samples but, whatever the
reason, the conductivity in these copolymers is very
poor in comparison with the sulphonate anion samples.

As totally “dryÏ, small-molecule, liquid-free polymer elec-
trolytes appear to have limitations to their performance,
it may be necessary to concede that some kind of “wetÏ
or more Ñuid system is required to provide better ion-
ization conditions and concurrently lead to an increase
in ion mobility and/or give a lower temperature depen-
dence of conductivity. The latter requirement is an
important factor to consider in device manufacture
Fig. 11. Comparison of conductivities between copolymer 6
and 9 and the homopolymer of 9 with
where the operating temperature range in the normal
environment can be quite large.
Movement towards this kind of more Ñuid system
usually means that the role of the polymer can change.
There will be a modiÐed inÑuence of the polymer chain
on conductivity in plasticized systems,29 whereas the
polymer may only act as a necessary, but inert, support
in, for example, gels.30 Two examples of polymer inert
Gels of b-cyclodextrins, crosslinked with epichloro-
(DMAc)/salt mixtures.31 The salts used were lithium,
sodium and potassium triÑates and the ratio of b-
cyclodextrin to DMAc/salt mixture was (5/95) wt%,
approximately 5 wt% being salt. This produced rubber-
like solids with “dryÏ surfaces which showed no evidence
of solvent leakage in spite of the high DMAc content
Conductivity measurements were carried out in the
temperature range 294È360 K and typical data are
plotted in Fig. 12. Under the conditions used the pot-
assium salt gives the highest conductivity.
The temperature dependence of the conductivity
follows the Arrhenius equation and the (d log p/dT )
values are quite low with room temperature conductiv-
ities of 10~4 S cm~1 rising to 1É5 ] 10~3 S cm~1 at
357 K for systems containing KCF3SO3 .
The results indicate that ion conduction is mainly
through DMAc with little or no inÑuence from the b-
Fig. 12. ConductivityÈtemperature
cyclodextrins. The latter have a hydrophobic interior to
the ring structure, that is in the form of a cup or torus,
which will complex with non-polar molecules rather
than with ions, as is the case with the crown ether struc-
ture. While there is some evidence of complexation of
Cu(II) ions by the hydroxyl groups on the exterior of
the ring,32 this may not be signiÐcant for the alkali
metal cations. Of the latter, the K` system exhibits
higher conductivities, suggesting that the
is more readily ionized and solvated by the DMAc.

similar to those for the b-cyclodextrin gels, which

perature conductivities of all the supported and gel

(400) systems, studied by Tsuchida
and Shigehara34,35 where the NaÐon also acts as a
The PE Ðlm is then a useful passive support produc-
ing thin (57 lm), strong conducting Ðlms when the pores
are loaded with a high dielectric medium containing a
Totally “dryÏ, ion conducting, comb-branch, polymer/
salt systems can be prepared that exhibit acceptable
levels of conductivity at ambient temperatures and are
mechanically strong. An increase in temperature leads
to a rise in the conductivity levels, but the temperature
dependence of conductivity is non-Arrhenius and falls
rapidly as the temperature decreases.
Ion conducted tour through some polyelectrolytes
Single cation conductors can be prepared with a
lower dependence of conductivity on temperature and
with conduction levels close to similar polymer electro-
lytes containing “freeÏ salts.
Even lower changes of conductivity with temperature
can be obtained when plasticized, gel or inert, polymer-
supported electrolytes are prepared. The (log pÈ1/T )
relations are now best described by an Arrhenius equa-
tion, and p is much less dependent on T . Such systems
show a number of attractive features, but must inevita-
bly be more prone to solvent leakage over a period of
time, which is a drawback not experienced by the “dryÏ

area, most of whom appear in the reference section.
